skills.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence and logical reasoning in biology
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced biology concepts for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(A)). Students should analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence and logical reasoning at an advanced level. Building on 9th grade foundations, 10th grade biology dives deeper into molecular biology, biochemistry, genetics, evolution, and ecology. Students apply scientific thinking to evaluate experimental evidence, distinguish between correlation and causation, and assess the validity of scientific claims. Key concepts: experimental design with appropriate controls, analyzing data using statistical reasoning, evaluating peer-reviewed scientific literature, understanding how scientific knowledge evolves through new evidence. Students should be able to construct and defend scientific arguments, identify logical fallacies in scientific reasoning, and apply the nature of science principles to current biological debates. Vocabulary: empirical evidence, logical reasoning, peer review, replication, validity, reliability, statistical significance, control group, experimental group, bias, scientific literacy, critical analysis. Common misconceptions: science proves things absolutely (science provides evidence-based explanations subject to revision); a theory is just an opinion.

skills.teks_description: Relate functions of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids to cellular structure and function
skills.prompt: This topic covers biochemistry for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.5(A)). Students should relate the functions of biological macromolecules to cellular structure and function. Four major macromolecules: carbohydrates (sugars and starches, quick energy and structural support; monomers: monosaccharides), lipids (fats, oils, waxes; long-term energy storage, cell membrane structure, insulation; not true polymers), proteins (enzymes, structural components, transport, defense; monomers: amino acids linked by peptide bonds), nucleic acids (DNA and RNA; store and transmit genetic information; monomers: nucleotides). Enzymes: biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions by lowering activation energy; substrate-specific (lock-and-key model or induced fit); affected by temperature, pH, and substrate concentration; denaturation occurs when enzyme shape is altered. Key concepts: dehydration synthesis builds polymers (releases water), hydrolysis breaks polymers (uses water), structure determines function, functional groups determine properties. Vocabulary: macromolecule, monomer, polymer, carbohydrate, lipid, protein, nucleic acid, enzyme, substrate, active site, denaturation, activation energy, dehydration synthesis, hydrolysis, peptide bond, amino acid.

skills.teks_description: Explain the cell cycle significance including DNA replication and mitosis stages
skills.prompt: This topic covers cellular reproduction for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.6(A)). Students should explain the cell cycle including DNA replication and stages of mitosis. Cell cycle: interphase (G1 growth, S DNA synthesis, G2 preparation) followed by M phase (mitosis + cytokinesis). Mitosis stages: prophase (chromosomes condense, spindle forms), metaphase (chromosomes align at cell equator), anaphase (sister chromatids separate toward poles), telophase (nuclear envelopes reform), cytokinesis (cytoplasm divides). Mitosis result: two genetically identical diploid daughter cells. Meiosis: two divisions producing four genetically unique haploid gametes. Meiosis I: homologous chromosomes separate (crossing over and independent assortment increase genetic diversity). Meiosis II: sister chromatids separate (like mitosis). Key concepts: cell cycle checkpoints and regulation, cancer as uncontrolled cell division, comparison of mitosis and meiosis, significance of genetic diversity in meiosis. Vocabulary: cell cycle, interphase, mitosis, meiosis, cytokinesis, chromosome, chromatid, centromere, spindle, diploid, haploid, homologous, crossing over, independent assortment, gamete.

skills.teks_description: Identify DNA changes and evaluate their significance
skills.prompt: This topic covers genetics for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.7(C)). Students should identify DNA changes and evaluate their significance. DNA mutations: point mutations (substitution, insertion, deletion of single nucleotides), frameshift mutations (insertions or deletions that shift the reading frame), chromosomal mutations (deletion, duplication, inversion, translocation of chromosome segments). Effects: silent (no amino acid change due to codon degeneracy), missense (different amino acid), nonsense (premature stop codon), frameshift (often severely changes protein). DNA replication errors: most are corrected by proofreading enzymes, but some persist as mutations. Mutagens: agents that increase mutation rate (UV radiation, chemicals, certain viruses). Gene regulation: not all genes are expressed in all cells; regulation occurs at multiple levels (transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, post-translational). Key concepts: mutations as source of genetic variation for evolution, beneficial vs. harmful vs. neutral mutations, relationship between genotype and phenotype. Vocabulary: mutation, point mutation, frameshift, substitution, insertion, deletion, mutagen, gene expression, gene regulation, silent mutation, missense, nonsense, chromosomal mutation.

skills.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers molecular biology and biotechnology for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should discuss advanced biotechnology techniques and their applications. Building on 9th grade: deeper understanding of PCR (thermal cycling details, primer design, applications in COVID testing and forensics), gel electrophoresis (DNA fingerprinting, RFLP analysis, forensic identification), genetic engineering (CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology, gene therapy for genetic disorders, transgenic organisms). Advanced concepts: genomics (study of entire genomes, Human Genome Project), proteomics (study of all proteins), bioinformatics (computational analysis of biological data), stem cell research and applications. Applications: personalized medicine, pharmacogenomics, agricultural biotechnology, environmental biotechnology (bioremediation), synthetic biology. Ethical dimensions: genetic privacy and discrimination (GINA Act), designer babies debate, GMO labeling, patent issues on genetic material, equitable access to genetic technologies. Vocabulary: PCR, gel electrophoresis, CRISPR, gene therapy, transgenic, genomics, proteomics, bioinformatics, stem cell, recombinant DNA, vector, plasmid, restriction enzyme, bioethics.

skills.teks_description: Analyze how natural selection leads to speciation
skills.prompt: This topic covers evolution for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.10(C)). Students should analyze how natural selection leads to speciation. Speciation: formation of new species; occurs when populations become reproductively isolated. Types: allopatric speciation (geographic isolation separates populations, e.g., mountain range, river), sympatric speciation (reproductive isolation within same area, e.g., polyploidy in plants, habitat differentiation). Reproductive isolation mechanisms: prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic) and postzygotic (hybrid inviability, infertility, breakdown). Mechanisms of evolution: natural selection, genetic drift (random changes in allele frequency, greater effect in small populations), gene flow (migration introduces alleles), mutation (creates new alleles), sexual selection. Evidence for evolution: fossil record (transitional fossils), comparative anatomy (homologous structures), molecular biology (DNA sequence comparisons, molecular clocks), biogeography, embryology, direct observation (bacterial resistance). Vocabulary: speciation, reproductive isolation, allopatric, sympatric, genetic drift, gene flow, mutation, natural selection, sexual selection, transitional fossil, molecular clock, adaptive radiation, convergent evolution, divergent evolution.

skills.skill: Ecology (energy flow, nutrient cycles, population dynamics)
skills.teks_description: Analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels
skills.prompt: This topic covers ecology for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.13(B)). Students should analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels. Energy flow: unidirectional through ecosystems; producers convert solar energy to chemical energy, approximately 10% transfers to each successive trophic level, rest lost as heat. Ecological pyramids: energy (always upright), biomass (usually upright), numbers (can be inverted). Biogeochemical cycles: carbon cycle (photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion, ocean absorption), nitrogen cycle (fixation by bacteria, nitrification, assimilation, denitrification), phosphorus cycle (weathering of rocks, no atmospheric component), water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, runoff). Disruptions: excess CO2 from fossil fuels (enhanced greenhouse effect), nitrogen fertilizer runoff (eutrophication), deforestation (carbon release, habitat loss), pollution. Population dynamics: exponential vs. logistic growth, carrying capacity, density-dependent and density-independent limiting factors. Vocabulary: trophic level, energy pyramid, biomass, biogeochemical cycle, carbon cycle, nitrogen cycle, nitrogen fixation, eutrophication, carrying capacity, limiting factor, ecological footprint.

skills.teks_description: Explain carbon and nitrogen cycle significance and disruption consequences
skills.prompt: This topic covers human impact on ecosystems for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.13(C)). Students should explain the significance of carbon and nitrogen cycles and consequences of their disruption. Carbon cycle disruptions: burning fossil fuels releases stored carbon as CO2, deforestation reduces carbon sinks, cement production releases CO2. Consequences: enhanced greenhouse effect, global warming, ocean acidification (CO2 dissolves in seawater forming carbonic acid, lowering pH), coral bleaching. Nitrogen cycle disruptions: synthetic fertilizers (Haber-Bosch process) add reactive nitrogen, fossil fuel combustion produces NOx. Consequences: eutrophication (excess nutrients cause algal blooms, decomposition depletes oxygen creating dead zones), acid rain (NOx contributes), groundwater contamination (nitrates in drinking water). Other human impacts: habitat destruction, pollution (air, water, soil), invasive species introduction, overexploitation of resources, biodiversity loss. Solutions: renewable energy, conservation, sustainable agriculture, pollution control, protected areas, international agreements. Vocabulary: carbon sink, carbon source, greenhouse effect, ocean acidification, eutrophication, dead zone, acid rain, nitrogen fixation, denitrification, biodiversity loss, sustainability, conservation.

skills.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense
skills.prompt: This topic covers human body systems in detail for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should analyze how animal body systems interact for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense at a deeper level. System interactions: circulatory + respiratory (gas exchange at lungs and tissues, oxygen-hemoglobin binding), digestive + circulatory (nutrient absorption in small intestine villi, transport via blood), nervous + endocrine (neuroendocrine integration, hypothalamus-pituitary axis), muscular + skeletal (movement via antagonistic muscle pairs, lever systems). Homeostasis: thermoregulation (nervous system detects, blood vessels dilate/constrict, sweat glands activate), blood glucose regulation (insulin lowers, glucagon raises — negative feedback), blood pressure regulation. Immune system detail: innate (barriers, inflammation, phagocytes) vs. adaptive (B cells/antibodies, T cells/cell-mediated, memory cells). Reproduction: hormonal control of reproductive cycles, fertilization, embryonic development basics. Vocabulary: homeostasis, negative feedback, positive feedback, hormone, receptor, effector, neurotransmitter, synapse, antibody, antigen, B cell, T cell, villi, alveoli, nephron, hemoglobin, antagonistic muscles.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in chemistry
skills.prompt: This topic covers chemistry foundations for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.4(A)). Students should apply analytical and critical thinking skills to evaluate chemical phenomena using empirical evidence. At the 10th grade level, chemistry builds on foundational concepts with deeper mathematical analysis and more complex problem-solving. Students should: design experiments with appropriate controls to test chemical hypotheses, analyze quantitative data from chemical investigations, evaluate the validity of chemical models (atomic models, bonding theories), understand how scientific understanding of chemistry has evolved historically (Dalton, Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr, quantum model). Key concepts: distinguishing between observations and inferences, identifying sources of experimental error, understanding precision vs. accuracy, using significant figures correctly, applying dimensional analysis. Students should connect microscopic models (atomic and molecular) to macroscopic observations (physical and chemical properties). Vocabulary: empirical, qualitative, quantitative, precision, accuracy, significant figures, dimensional analysis, model, theory, law, observation, inference, hypothesis, systematic error, random error.

skills.teks_description: Perform calculations involving heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat
skills.prompt: This topic covers thermochemistry and thermodynamics for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.13(D)). Students should perform calculations involving heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat. Core equation: q = mcΔT where q = heat energy (joules), m = mass (grams), c = specific heat capacity (J/g°C), ΔT = temperature change (Tf - Ti). Water's specific heat: 4.184 J/g°C (high compared to most substances). Calorimetry: measuring heat changes using qreleased = -qabsorbed. Exothermic vs. endothermic: exothermic releases heat (ΔH < 0, feels warm), endothermic absorbs heat (ΔH > 0, feels cold). Enthalpy changes: represent energy of reactions; Hess's law allows calculating ΔH for reactions by summing steps. Energy diagrams: show activation energy, reactant/product energy levels, and catalyst effects. Key concepts: conservation of energy, system vs. surroundings, specific heat determines how quickly substances heat up. Vocabulary: thermochemistry, enthalpy, specific heat, calorimetry, exothermic, endothermic, activation energy, catalyst, Hess's law, joule, calorie, heat capacity, system, surroundings.

skills.teks_description: Classify processes as exothermic or endothermic and represent energy changes
skills.prompt: This topic covers chemical kinetics and equilibrium for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.13(C)). Students should classify processes as exothermic or endothermic and understand reaction energetics. Kinetics: study of reaction rates. Factors affecting rate: temperature (higher = faster), concentration (higher = faster), surface area (more = faster), catalysts (lower activation energy), nature of reactants. Collision theory: effective collisions require sufficient energy and proper orientation. Equilibrium: dynamic state where forward and reverse rates are equal; concentrations remain constant. Le Chatelier's principle: system responds to stress by shifting to counteract it — adding reactant shifts right, removing product shifts right, increasing temperature shifts toward endothermic direction, pressure changes affect gases. Equilibrium expression: Keq = [products]/[reactants] with coefficients as exponents; large Keq favors products, small Keq favors reactants. Thermochemical equations: include ΔH values; can be manipulated (reverse = change sign, multiply = multiply ΔH). Vocabulary: kinetics, rate, collision theory, activation energy, catalyst, equilibrium, Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constant, exothermic, endothermic, thermochemical equation, dynamic equilibrium.

skills.teks_description: Analyze substance properties regarding intramolecular and intermolecular forces
skills.prompt: This topic covers introductory organic chemistry for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.7(D)). Students should analyze substance properties based on intramolecular and intermolecular forces. Carbon chemistry: carbon forms 4 covalent bonds, creating diverse molecular structures (chains, branches, rings). Hydrocarbons: alkanes (single bonds, saturated), alkenes (double bonds), alkynes (triple bonds). Functional groups: hydroxyl (-OH, alcohols), carboxyl (-COOH, carboxylic acids), amino (-NH2, amines), carbonyl (C=O, aldehydes/ketones). Intramolecular forces: covalent bonds within molecules; bond energy, bond length, and polarity depend on atoms involved. Intermolecular forces: forces between molecules determining physical properties; London dispersion (all molecules, increases with molecular size), dipole-dipole (polar molecules), hydrogen bonding (H bonded to F, O, or N — strongest intermolecular force in molecular compounds). Property trends: boiling point, melting point, and viscosity increase with stronger intermolecular forces; solubility follows like dissolves like. Vocabulary: organic, hydrocarbon, functional group, intramolecular, intermolecular, London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bond, polarity, saturated, unsaturated, isomer.

skills.teks_description: Describe alpha, beta, and gamma radioactive decay characteristics using balanced nuclear equations
skills.prompt: This topic covers nuclear chemistry for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.14(A)). Students should describe radioactive decay types and write balanced nuclear equations. Types of radioactive decay: alpha decay (emission of helium-4 nucleus, mass number decreases by 4, atomic number by 2; heavy elements), beta decay (neutron converts to proton + electron; atomic number increases by 1, mass unchanged; neutron-rich nuclei), gamma decay (emission of high-energy photon; no change in mass or atomic number; accompanies other decay types). Balancing nuclear equations: conserve mass number (top) and atomic number (bottom) on both sides. Half-life: time for half of radioactive sample to decay; N = N0(1/2)^(t/t1/2). Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits (e.g., U-235), releases large energy, basis of nuclear power. Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine (e.g., hydrogen to helium), even more energy, powers the sun. Applications: medical imaging and therapy, carbon-14 dating, nuclear power, smoke detectors. Vocabulary: radioactive decay, alpha particle, beta particle, gamma ray, half-life, nuclear equation, fission, fusion, isotope, radioisotope, transmutation, chain reaction, nuclear binding energy.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in physics
skills.prompt: This topic covers physics foundations for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.4(A)). At the 10th grade level, students build upon introductory physics with more rigorous mathematical treatment and deeper conceptual understanding. Students should analyze and critique physical explanations using quantitative evidence, vector analysis, and mathematical modeling. Key areas of study: advanced kinematics (two-dimensional motion, projectile problems), dynamics (complex force scenarios, inclined planes, friction), energy (conservation with multiple energy types), waves (standing waves, interference), and introduction to electricity and magnetism. Mathematical skills applied: trigonometry for vector decomposition, algebra for multi-step problem solving, graphical analysis for interpreting motion and force diagrams. Students should design experiments, analyze data, calculate percent error, and evaluate whether results support or refute hypotheses. Scientific models: evaluate limitations of physical models and understand when simplifications (frictionless surfaces, point masses) are appropriate. Vocabulary: vector, scalar, component, resolution, model, idealization, percent error, systematic error, experimental design, quantitative analysis.

skills.teks_description: Describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion in two dimensions
skills.prompt: This topic covers two-dimensional motion for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion. Projectile motion: combines constant horizontal velocity (no horizontal acceleration) with vertical free fall (a = g = 9.8 m/s² downward); trajectory is parabolic. Horizontal and vertical components are independent. Key equations: horizontal (x = v0x × t), vertical (y = v0y × t - ½gt², vy = v0y - gt). Launch angle affects range: 45° gives maximum range (no air resistance). Uniform circular motion: constant speed in a circle; velocity direction constantly changes (acceleration exists). Centripetal acceleration: ac = v²/r, directed toward center. Centripetal force: Fc = mv²/r, the net inward force (not a separate force — provided by tension, gravity, friction, etc.). Period: T = 2πr/v. Key concepts: independence of horizontal and vertical motions, time is the linking variable in projectile problems, circular motion is accelerated even at constant speed. Vocabulary: projectile, trajectory, range, launch angle, centripetal acceleration, centripetal force, uniform circular motion, period, frequency, tangential velocity.

skills.teks_description: Calculate force effects using free body diagrams and Newton's second law
skills.prompt: This topic covers applied forces and Newton's laws for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.5(F)). Students should solve complex force problems using free body diagrams and Newton's second law. Advanced applications: inclined planes (resolve gravity into parallel and perpendicular components: F_parallel = mg sin θ, F_perpendicular = mg cos θ), connected objects (multiple objects with tension), Atwood machines (two masses over a pulley), objects in equilibrium (ΣF = 0 in both x and y). Friction: static friction fs ≤ μsN (prevents motion), kinetic friction fk = μkN (opposes motion); calculate normal force on inclined planes as N = mg cos θ. Multi-body problems: draw separate FBDs for each object, write ΣF = ma for each, use constraints (same acceleration for connected objects, same tension in massless rope). Key concepts: choosing optimal coordinate system, handling multiple forces in 2D, applying Newton's laws to real-world scenarios (elevator problems, towing, banking). Vocabulary: free body diagram, normal force, friction, tension, inclined plane, component, net force, equilibrium, coefficient of friction, constraint, system, acceleration.

skills.teks_description: Calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers momentum and impulse for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.7(D)). Students should calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems. Momentum: p = mv, vector quantity (has direction), measured in kg·m/s. Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp, the change in momentum caused by a force over time. Conservation of momentum: total momentum of an isolated system remains constant (Σpi = Σpf). Collisions: elastic (momentum AND kinetic energy conserved — billiard balls), inelastic (momentum conserved, KE not — car crashes), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together, maximum KE loss). Collision equations: m1v1i + m2v2i = m1v1f + m2v2f (general); m1v1i + m2v2i = (m1+m2)vf (perfectly inelastic). Applications: car safety (airbags and crumple zones increase impact time to reduce force), rocket propulsion (expelling gas creates thrust by conservation of momentum), sports (follow-through increases impulse). Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, isolated system, recoil, kinetic energy, impact force, impulse-momentum theorem.

skills.teks_description: Calculate gravitational force magnitude using Newton's law of universal gravitation
skills.prompt: This topic covers circular motion and gravitation for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.5(H)). Students should calculate gravitational force using Newton's law of universal gravitation. Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = Gm1m2/r², where G = 6.674 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg², m1 and m2 are the two masses, r is the distance between centers. Gravitational field strength: g = GM/r², equals 9.8 m/s² at Earth's surface. Orbital motion: gravitational force provides centripetal force; setting GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital velocity v = √(GM/r). Weight varies by location (on Moon, on other planets) but mass stays constant. Key concepts: inverse-square relationship (doubling distance reduces force to 1/4), gravitational force is always attractive, all masses attract all other masses, weightlessness in orbit is really free fall. Kepler's laws: (1) elliptical orbits, (2) equal areas in equal times, (3) T² proportional to r³. Vocabulary: gravitational force, gravitational constant, universal gravitation, gravitational field, orbital velocity, weight, mass, inverse-square law, Kepler's laws, satellite, orbit, free fall, escape velocity.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and transformations
skills.prompt: This topic covers energy conservation for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply the work-energy theorem and conservation of energy to solve problems. Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔKE = ½mv²f - ½mv²i; net work on an object equals its change in kinetic energy. Conservation of mechanical energy: KE1 + PE1 = KE2 + PE2 (when only conservative forces act); ½mv²1 + mgh1 = ½mv²2 + mgh2. Including non-conservative forces: KE1 + PE1 + Wnc = KE2 + PE2, where Wnc accounts for friction (negative) or applied forces. Energy types: kinetic (½mv²), gravitational potential (mgh), elastic potential (½kx²). Power: P = W/t = Fv, rate of energy transfer (watts). Problem-solving strategy: identify system, choose initial and final states, apply conservation or work-energy theorem. Applications: roller coasters, pendulums, projectiles, springs, sliding with friction. Key concepts: choosing reference point for PE, energy is scalar, energy bar charts as visual tools. Vocabulary: work-energy theorem, conservation of energy, kinetic energy, potential energy, mechanical energy, conservative force, non-conservative force, power, watt, joule, dissipation.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
skills.prompt: This topic covers electricity and magnetism for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze and construct series and parallel circuits. Circuit fundamentals: current I = Q/t (charge flow rate, amperes), voltage V (potential difference, volts), resistance R (opposition, ohms). Ohm's law: V = IR. Series circuits: same current through all components, voltages add, resistances add (Req = R1+R2). Parallel circuits: same voltage across branches, currents add, 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Kirchhoff's laws: junction rule (currents in = currents out), loop rule (voltages around loop sum to zero). Magnetism basics: magnetic fields from magnets and current-carrying wires, electromagnetic induction (changing magnetic field induces current), applications (motors, generators, transformers). Key concepts: circuit diagrams and symbols, measuring with ammeter (series) and voltmeter (parallel), household wiring, electrical safety, relationship between electricity and magnetism. Vocabulary: current, voltage, resistance, Ohm's law, series, parallel, circuit, ammeter, voltmeter, power, magnetic field, electromagnetic induction, Kirchhoff's laws.

skills.teks_description: Compare electromagnetic spectrum applications including radio telescopes and x-rays
skills.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic waves for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.8(E)). Students should compare applications across the electromagnetic spectrum. Electromagnetic waves: transverse waves consisting of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to propagation direction; travel at c = 3 × 10^8 m/s in vacuum; do not require a medium. EM spectrum (increasing frequency): radio (communication, radio telescopes for astronomy), microwaves (cooking, cell phones, radar), infrared (thermal imaging, night vision, remote controls), visible light (human vision, ~400-700 nm, ROY G BIV), ultraviolet (sterilization, black lights, causes sunburn), X-rays (medical imaging, airport security, crystallography), gamma rays (cancer treatment, food sterilization, nuclear processes). Wave relationships: c = fλ, E = hf (higher frequency = higher energy). Applications in science: radio telescopes detect celestial radio waves, spectroscopy identifies elements by emission/absorption spectra, medical imaging uses X-rays and MRI. Vocabulary: electromagnetic wave, electromagnetic spectrum, frequency, wavelength, radio wave, microwave, infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma ray, photon, spectroscopy, speed of light.
